criteria.criterion_statement: The student pauses to notice body signals and emotional activation, names what they are feeling, and uses a practised calming strategy to return to learning, without needing step-by-step adult direction.
criteria.criterion_label: [Band A] Self-Awareness & Regulation (dispositional observation)
criteria.decomposition_rationale: This produces one criterion as the three components (noticing body signals, naming feelings, using strategies) form an integrated self-regulation process observable as a single dispositional behaviour at this developmental level.
observation_indicators: The student pauses to name a feeling ("I'm frustrated", "I'm tired") before reacting when emotional activation rises, on most occasions visible to the teacher.
The student selects a practised calming strategy (deep breath, water, movement break, quiet corner) without needing step-by-step adult direction.
The student returns to the learning activity after using a strategy, rather than requiring an adult to redirect them each time.
The student names body signals (tight tummy, hot face, fast heart) as they arise, not only after the moment has passed.
confusable_behaviours: Compliance with adult-directed calming (following an instruction to "take a breath") mistaken for self-regulation. Distinction: the disposition is visible when the adult has not prompted and the student initiates the pause themselves.

Emotional suppression (freezing and going quiet) mistaken for calming. Distinction: suppression is visible as withdrawal of affect; regulation is visible as return to engagement after a brief self-directed pause.
absence_indicators: In classroom moments of frustration, the student's first response is consistently externalised (shouting, tears, hitting the desk) with no observable pause.
The student relies on the same adult for every regulation episode, with no transfer to self-directed use of practised strategies.
conversation_prompts: Tell me about a time this week when you felt a big feeling. What did your body do?
When you use your calming corner or your breathing, what do you notice changes?
Tell me about a time a feeling surprised you — what was it, and what was going on?

criteria.criterion_statement: The student notices recurring personal triggers, chooses from a practised repertoire of regulation strategies before activation escalates, and does so across more than one setting.
criteria.criterion_label: [Band B] Self-Awareness & Regulation (dispositional observation)
criteria.decomposition_rationale: This is a single dispositional behaviour combining trigger awareness with strategic response selection, observable as one integrated competency at this developmental level.
observation_indicators: The student names a recurring personal trigger (end-of-morning hunger, group work with a specific peer, maths-problem frustration) in their own words, not only after the fact.
The student chooses from a practised repertoire of strategies (not only the single strategy they learned first) based on what the trigger is.
The student applies a regulation strategy before activation escalates in at least two different settings (classroom work, playground, group work).
The student comments on strategies that did or didn't work for them without adult prompting.
confusable_behaviours: Routine compliance (using the same strategy every time because it is the one the class was taught) mistaken for trigger-responsive regulation. Distinction: look for the student picking a strategy that fits the situation, not applying a default.

Avoidance (deliberately steering around a known trigger) mistaken for regulation. Distinction: avoidance reduces exposure; regulation shows up when the student stays in the situation and manages their state.
absence_indicators: The student can name strategies abstractly but only applies them with adult reminder, even when a known trigger is active.
The student's triggers recur predictably and the response pattern remains unchanged across weeks (same escalation, same adult intervention, no emerging self-action).
conversation_prompts: What's something that sets you off at school — and how do you usually know it's starting?
Tell me about a time you caught yourself early this month. What did you do?
Which of your calming strategies feels most like it actually works — and which one doesn't?

criteria.criterion_statement: The student applies a regulation strategy in a genuinely challenging situation and afterwards explains what worked and what didn't, adjusting their choice of strategy based on what they have learned.
criteria.criterion_label: [Band C] Self-Awareness & Regulation (dispositional observation)
criteria.decomposition_rationale: This represents one integrated dispositional behaviour combining strategy application with reflective evaluation, observable as a single competency pattern.
observation_indicators: The student applies a regulation strategy in a genuinely challenging moment (not a rehearsed low-stakes practice), on multiple occasions across a term.
Afterwards — spontaneously or on a brief prompt — the student explains what worked and what didn't, rather than only reporting "I used my strategy".
The student adjusts choice of strategy based on what they have learned from earlier attempts, not by sticking to one default.
The student distinguishes strategies that genuinely helped from those that felt like they should have helped but didn't.
confusable_behaviours: Post-hoc narration of compliance ("I did the breathing") mistaken for strategic reflection. Distinction: the disposition shows when the student can point to something that didn't work and explain why, not only successes.

Rehearsal fluency (being able to describe strategies in a reflection lesson) mistaken for in-the-moment application. Distinction: the evidence is visible in the middle of a hard situation, not in planned reflection.
absence_indicators: The student performs regulation language convincingly in low-stakes settings but reverts to pre-strategy patterns the moment genuine difficulty lands.
The student's post-hoc reflections stay at the level of "it worked" / "it didn't work" with no specificity about what or why.
conversation_prompts: When do you find it easiest to use a regulation strategy, and when does it feel hardest?
Tell me about a time a strategy you expected to work didn't — what happened?
How has the set of strategies you rely on changed across this year?

criteria.criterion_statement: The student applies different regulation strategies across different settings, explains without prompt why one fits one setting and not another, and names a specific change they are going to try next.
criteria.criterion_label: [Band D] Self-Awareness & Regulation (dispositional observation)
criteria.decomposition_rationale: This is a single dispositional criterion as it represents one integrated capability - the ability to maintain strategic emotional regulation under stress while engaging in metacognitive reflection across contexts.
observation_indicators: The student applies different regulation strategies across different settings (academic task pressure, peer friction, home-context reports), not a single default everywhere.
The student explains — without adult prompt — why one strategy fits one setting and not another (what the context allows, what the activation pattern is).
The student names a specific, concrete change to their regulation practice they are going to try next, grounded in what they have learned.
The student adjusts strategies when a previously reliable one stops working in a new context.
confusable_behaviours: Context-matching as performance (using the "right" strategy because that's what the adult expects in this room) mistaken for authentic context-sensitivity. Distinction: the disposition shows in the student's own reasoning about fit, not in conformity to classroom norms.

Sophisticated talk about regulation in reflection without corresponding behaviour change across contexts mistaken for integration. Distinction: the student's practice in home-context and peer-context settings should register the growth, not only in supervised school settings.
absence_indicators: The student uses one consistent strategy in all contexts, with no adjustment when context or available resources change.
The student can discuss context-sensitivity fluently but reports from parents and other settings show the same reactivity pattern the student had a year ago.
conversation_prompts: Where does your regulation feel most reliable and where does it still feel effortful?
How has your approach shifted between school, home, and friendships this year?
What's the next change you're thinking about making — and what's making you think it's time?

criteria.criterion_statement: The student notices and names feelings in others, and offers a kind word or specific helpful action in response, without adult direction.
criteria.criterion_label: [Band A] Social Awareness & Empathy (dispositional observation)
criteria.decomposition_rationale: This is a single dispositional criterion as it captures one integrated empathetic behavior combining emotional recognition with caring response action.
observation_indicators: The student names a feeling another child is showing (sad, worried, left out) in their own words, not only their own feelings.
The student offers a kind word or a specific helpful action (sitting with, inviting to play, sharing) in response, without adult direction.
The student notices the emotion of a quieter or less visible child, not only children who are loudly upset.
confusable_behaviours: Rule-following (reciting "use kind words") mistaken for empathic noticing. Distinction: empathic response is triggered by what the student sees in the other child; rule-following is triggered by an adult instruction or routine.
Performative sweetness (being kind because it earns adult approval) mistaken for empathy. Distinction: empathic action usually happens without an audience watching.
absence_indicators: When another child is visibly upset nearby, the student proceeds without apparent notice, even in small settings where the signal is clear.
The student's kind actions are tightly tied to being observed by an adult — the behaviour drops when adult attention moves away.
conversation_prompts: Tell me about a time this week when you noticed someone felt sad or worried. How did you know?
Tell me about a time you made somebody feel better. What did you do?
When you see someone on their own at break, what goes through your head?

criteria.criterion_statement: The student takes a peer's perspective in their own words, asks before acting on assumption about what the peer needs, and accepts a "no" without pushing.
criteria.criterion_label: [Band B] Social Awareness & Empathy (dispositional observation)
criteria.decomposition_rationale: This is a single dispositional behaviour combining perspective-taking with respectful helping practices, observable as one integrated social skill.
observation_indicators: The student describes a situation from another person's perspective in their own words (not only restating what the other said).
The student asks "do you want help?" (or an equivalent check-in) before acting on their assumption about what the other person needs.
The student accepts a "no" or "I'm fine" from a peer without pushing, continuing to help anyway, or taking the refusal personally.
The student spontaneously verifies rather than guesses when emotional signals are ambiguous, on multiple occasions.
confusable_behaviours: Assuming and acting (bringing a peer something without asking because "I thought you'd want it") mistaken for empathic responsiveness. Distinction: the disposition shows in checking first, especially when the "help" would otherwise be directive.
Ritualised asking ("do you want help?" as an automatic phrase) mistaken for genuine check-in. Distinction: the disposition includes actually listening to and acting on the answer.
absence_indicators: The student defaults to their own interpretation of what a peer needs and is visibly surprised or upset when the peer's version differs.
The student pushes past a "no" when it contradicts their picture of helping, or becomes hurt when help is declined.
conversation_prompts: Tell me about a time someone felt differently than you expected about something — what did you learn?
When is it easiest to ask how someone is feeling, and when is it hardest?
Tell me about a time a friend said they were fine but you thought they weren't. What did you do?

criteria.criterion_statement: The student attends to multiple group members during shared work, checks in directly when signals are unclear, and adjusts their own actions to match what the other has said they need.
criteria.criterion_label: [Band C] Social Awareness & Empathy (dispositional observation)
criteria.decomposition_rationale: This is a single dispositional criterion as it represents one integrated social behavior of awareness and responsive adjustment within group contexts.
observation_indicators: The student attends to multiple members of a working group (not only the loudest or closest), tracking who is engaged, who is withdrawing, who is confused.
The student checks in with a direct question when signals are unclear, rather than acting on assumption.
The student adjusts their own actions to match what the other person has said they need, on multiple occasions.
The student attends to what a peer is doing (withdrawn posture, silence, not participating) as well as to what they say, when reading emotional state.
confusable_behaviours: Centring the most expressive peer (attending well to the person who says the most) mistaken for broad social awareness. Distinction: the disposition shows in attention to the quieter group members too.
Checking-in-then-doing-what-I-was-going-to-do (ritual check-in followed by the student's own plan anyway) mistaken for responsive empathy. Distinction: the action actually shifts based on the answer.
absence_indicators: In group work, the student consistently over-indexes on one or two peers and loses track of the others, without noticing the pattern.
The student asks questions but proceeds without adjusting behaviour to the responses, indicating the asking is ritualised.
conversation_prompts: When do you find it easiest to tell what a group member is feeling, and when do you find it hardest?
Tell me about a time you adjusted what you were doing because of what someone else needed — what shifted?
How do you know when someone in your group is fading out without saying so?

criteria.criterion_statement: After a conflict, the student listens without interrupting, names their own contribution to the situation, and proposes a next step that accounts for the other person's position as well as their own.
criteria.criterion_label: [Band D] Social Awareness & Empathy (dispositional observation)
criteria.decomposition_rationale: This is a single dispositional behavior requiring integrated demonstration of listening, accountability, and collaborative problem-solving as one coherent relationship repair process.
observation_indicators: After a conflict, the student listens to the other party without interrupting, even when they disagree.
The student names their own contribution to the conflict (what they did, said, or assumed) without only narrating the other person's faults.
The student proposes a next step that accounts for the other person's position — not only a resolution that protects their own.
The student treats repair as part of the relationship, not as an admission of being wholly at fault.
confusable_behaviours: Apology performance ("I said sorry") mistaken for repair. Distinction: repair includes listening, naming contribution, and proposing change; apology without those elements is conflict-closure, not repair.
Self-blame as a way to end the conflict quickly, mistaken for accountability. Distinction: repair names a specific contribution; self-blame flattens into "it was all my fault so let's move on".
absence_indicators: After a conflict, the student's account consistently places the cause entirely on the other party, with no observable noticing of their own role.
The student avoids conflict rather than repair, absenting themselves from a peer or context until the situation is forgotten.
conversation_prompts: Tell me about a time you and a friend disagreed and then repaired it — what was the hardest part?
When has it been easiest to own your part in a conflict, and when has it felt hardest?
What do you notice about how you react right after a disagreement — what's the move you want to practise?

criteria.criterion_statement: I can complete a short sustained-attention task using my senses to stay on it.
criteria.criterion_label: Sensory-guided sustained attention

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band A.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I start a short sustained-attention task but lose track of it before the end because I am not yet using a sense to anchor my focus.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I complete the task while using a sense to stay on it for part of it, but my focus slips when the task gets longer or when a second sense is pulling at me.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can complete a short sustained-attention task using my senses to stay on it.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I choose which sense to use based on what the task actually needs (eyes for reading, ears for listening) rather than relying on the same sense every time.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: This addresses the attentional capacity component of the learning target.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can describe one thing I did when my attention wandered during a task.
criteria.criterion_label: Attention-return action description

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band A.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I notice my attention wandered but cannot yet say what I did next, or I say only that I started again without naming a specific action.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I name an action I took after my attention wandered, but my description is vague or mixes up what I did with what the teacher asked me to do.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can describe one thing I did when my attention wandered during a task.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I describe the action I took and notice that I tried it on my own, because I caught my attention wandering before a reminder came.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: This addresses the metacognitive awareness component which involves different cognitive processes than sustaining attention itself.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can use a practised attention strategy during a task.
criteria.criterion_label: Apply practised attention strategy

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band B.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I name a practised attention strategy but do not use it during the task, or I try it only after being reminded.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I apply the strategy once during the task but do not return to it when my attention drifts a second or third time.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can use a practised attention strategy during a task.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I notice that one practised strategy is not working today and switch to a different one from my practised set without being prompted.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Two criteria are needed because using a strategy and reflecting on focus factors represent distinct cognitive operations that can be independently demonstrated.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can describe, in writing or conversation, what made it easier or harder to stay focused.
criteria.criterion_label: Reflect on focus factors in writing or speech

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band B.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I name the task I did but not what made focusing easier or harder, or I only say 'it was hard' without pointing to anything specific.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I name one thing that helped or got in the way, but I do not pair both an easier and a harder factor, or I do not explain how the factor affected me.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can describe, in writing or conversation, what made it easier or harder to stay focused.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I notice that the same factor helps in one kind of task but gets in the way in another, and can say why.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Two criteria are needed because using a strategy and reflecting on focus factors represent distinct cognitive operations that can be independently demonstrated.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can evaluate how an attention strategy affected my focus during a specific task in a written reflection.
criteria.criterion_label: Strategy impact evaluation in writing

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band C.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: My reflection names the strategy and the task but not how the strategy actually affected my focus, or the evaluation is just 'it worked' with no specifics.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I write about how the strategy affected my focus but do not tie the effect to particular moments in the task, or I state an effect without the evidence for it.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can evaluate how an attention strategy affected my focus during a specific task in a written reflection.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: My written evaluation distinguishes what the strategy did from other things that were also shaping my focus that day.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: The band statement contains two distinct cognitive operations: evaluating strategy effectiveness and explaining reasoning, which require separate assessment.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can explain why an attention strategy did or didn't help my focus, referencing the mechanism by which it works.
criteria.criterion_label: Strategy mechanism explanation

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band C.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I say whether the strategy helped but describe the outcome instead of the mechanism, or I use a mechanism word without explaining what it actually does.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I reference a mechanism (breathing slows arousal, chunking reduces load) but the explanation does not fully connect the mechanism to the specific effect I observed.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can explain why an attention strategy did or didn't help my focus, referencing the mechanism by which it works.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I explain why the same mechanism would work differently for a different kind of task or for someone in a different state.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: The band statement contains two distinct cognitive operations: evaluating strategy effectiveness and explaining reasoning, which require separate assessment.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can sustain attention during a challenging task.
criteria.criterion_label: Sustained attention under challenge

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band D.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I start a challenging task but my attention breaks down as soon as the challenge rises, and I do not hold focus long enough to make meaningful progress on the hard part.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I sustain attention through parts of the challenging task but lose it at specific predictable points (when difficulty spikes, when the work becomes ambiguous) and do not recover.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can sustain attention during a challenging task.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I sustain attention through the hard section of the task and notice in the moment which part of the challenge was threatening my focus.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Sustaining attention is a distinct cognitive operation from identifying distractors or applying strategies.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can identify in writing what pulled my attention away during a task.
criteria.criterion_label: Written distractor identification

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band D.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: My writing names that I got distracted but does not identify what pulled my attention away, or it names only 'other people' or 'my phone' without more detail.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I name a distractor but treat it as a one-off rather than the specific thing that pulls my attention most often, or I name an external distractor without naming the internal one.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can identify in writing what pulled my attention away during a task.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I distinguish between a distractor I could remove and one I need a strategy for because it will still be there.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Identifying distractors requires metacognitive awareness distinct from the other operations.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can describe the deliberate strategy I used to return my attention to the task.
criteria.criterion_label: Attention-return strategy description

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band D.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I describe that I returned to the task but not the deliberate strategy I used, or my description calls any response a strategy without showing intention.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I describe a deliberate strategy but do not show why I chose that one over another, or I describe a strategy that was prompted rather than chosen.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can describe the deliberate strategy I used to return my attention to the task.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I describe the strategy and say why this strategy fitted this particular distraction rather than another I could have used.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Strategic application of attention-return techniques is a separate cognitive operation requiring deliberate selection and execution of specific methods.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can retell a short experience orally or in drawing-plus-sentence format.
criteria.criterion_label: Experience retelling

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band A.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I tell or draw a fragment of the experience but my account misses either when it happened, who was there, or what actually took place.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: My retelling covers most of the experience but the order of events is unclear or a key moment is missing from the drawing or sentence.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can retell a short experience orally or in drawing-plus-sentence format.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: My retelling includes one small detail that would only be known to someone who was actually there, not just remembered from the surface of the event.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Retelling an experience and identifying learning from it are distinct cognitive operations requiring separate assessment.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can state one thing I learned from a short experience.
criteria.criterion_label: Learning identification

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band A.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I describe the experience but do not state what I learned, or I say 'it was fun' instead of naming a learning.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I state something I learned but it is a generic lesson like 'be careful' rather than connected to the specific experience I had.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can state one thing I learned from a short experience.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I state what I learned and point to the specific moment in the experience that taught it to me.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Retelling an experience and identifying learning from it are distinct cognitive operations requiring separate assessment.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can state one helpful and one harmful possible outcome for a choice before making my decision.
criteria.criterion_label: Outcome anticipation before decision

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band B.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I name only one side (either helpful or harmful) when anticipating outcomes for my choice, or I name both but the harmful outcome is not actually a possible outcome of this choice.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I name one helpful and one harmful outcome but one of them is generic ('something bad could happen') rather than specific to the choice I am about to make.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can state one helpful and one harmful possible outcome for a choice before making my decision.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I name outcomes that are actually possible for this specific choice, and say which outcome I think is more likely and why.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: This criterion captures the metacognitive 'stopping' behaviour that precedes the outcome analysis, which is a distinct observable action from identifying consequences.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can write one sentence about what happened after making my choice.
criteria.criterion_label: Post-decision reflection sentence

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band B.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: My sentence after the choice describes the choice itself rather than what happened as a result of it.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I write about what happened afterwards but do not distinguish between what was caused by my choice and what would have happened anyway.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can write one sentence about what happened after making my choice.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: My sentence names something that happened I did not anticipate when I made the choice.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: This criterion focuses on the cognitive skill of consequence prediction, which is a separate demonstrable ability from the metacognitive pause behaviour.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can compare three options for a real decision I face in a written format.
criteria.criterion_label: Written three-option comparison

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band C.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I name three options but my writing does not compare them — I describe each one in turn without putting them side by side on any criterion.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I compare the options on one dimension (cost, effort, fairness) but do not apply that criterion to all three, or I compare some pairs but not the full three-way.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can compare three options for a real decision I face in a written format.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: My comparison names a criterion that matters for this decision but is not obvious, and applies it to all three options.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Three distinct cognitive operations require separate assessment: comparing options involves analysis, stating choice requires decision-making, and giving value-based reasons involves justification.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can state my choice from the options I have compared.
criteria.criterion_label: State choice from comparison

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band C.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I state a choice but it is not drawn from the options I compared — I switch to a new option I had not put on the list.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I state a choice from the compared options but my response does not show that I considered and set aside the other options.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can state my choice from the options I have compared.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I state my choice and identify the specific condition that would make me pick a different option instead.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Three distinct cognitive operations require separate assessment: comparing options involves analysis, stating choice requires decision-making, and giving value-based reasons involves justification.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can give a reason for my choice that references a named personal value.
criteria.criterion_label: Named value-based reasoning

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band C.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I give a reason for my choice but it describes the outcome rather than referencing a personal value, or I name a value that was not actually in play in this choice.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I reference a value but do not name which specific value it is, or I use a value-word without making clear why it applied here.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can give a reason for my choice that references a named personal value.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I name which of two competing values I weighted more heavily in this choice and why.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Three distinct cognitive operations require separate assessment: comparing options involves analysis, stating choice requires decision-making, and giving value-based reasons involves justification.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can write a structured analysis of a real or realistic decision scenario that names the trade-offs involved — what is gained and lost across different options.
criteria.criterion_label: Written trade-off analysis

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band D.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: My analysis describes the decision scenario but does not name what is gained and lost in each option, or it names gains without naming what is given up.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I name trade-offs on one or two options but do not hold the trade-offs clearly parallel across the options, or I name costs without naming what they are paid for.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can write a structured analysis of a real or realistic decision scenario that names the trade-offs involved — what is gained and lost across different options.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I identify a trade-off where the loss on one option is not the same kind of thing as the loss on the other, so a simple pros/cons list would miss it.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Two distinct cognitive operations: explaining trade-offs requires analytical thinking about gains/losses, while justifying choices based on effects on people requires evaluative reasoning with empathy.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can consider how a decision affects at least two different stakeholders or affected parties.
criteria.criterion_label: Stakeholder impact consideration

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band D.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I name one stakeholder but not a second, or I list two stakeholders but describe effects only on myself.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I name how the decision affects two stakeholders but do not distinguish the different ways it affects each of them, treating the effect as generic.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can consider how a decision affects at least two different stakeholders or affected parties.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I identify how the two stakeholders' interests diverge and name which tension my decision accepts.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Two distinct cognitive operations: explaining trade-offs requires analytical thinking about gains/losses, while justifying choices based on effects on people requires evaluative reasoning with empathy.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can name my body's basic needs using a labelled diagram or sentence-frame reflection.
criteria.criterion_label: Basic body needs naming

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band A.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I name one body need (food or water or sleep) but not the range my body has, or I use the labelled diagram but copy labels without saying what they are about.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I name several body needs using the diagram or frame but do not connect them to what my body feels when a need is not met.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can name my body's basic needs using a labelled diagram or sentence-frame reflection.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I name a body need that is often overlooked in our diagram or frame (e.g. warmth, calm, movement) that I know my body also has.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Naming basic needs is a distinct cognitive act from practicing hygiene routines, requiring factual knowledge rather than procedural demonstration.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can follow a simple personal hygiene routine with supportive reminders.
criteria.criterion_label: Hygiene routine with reminders

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band A.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I complete parts of the hygiene routine when reminded at each step but cannot yet name the full sequence myself.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I name and follow most of the hygiene routine but skip one step or need a reminder at the same predictable point each time.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can follow a simple personal hygiene routine with supportive reminders.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I notice when a step of the routine needs adapting (e.g. washing hands harder after a mess, brushing longer after sweets) and adjust without a reminder.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Practicing hygiene routines involves different procedural skills from naming body needs, requiring physical demonstration rather than verbal knowledge.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can explain in writing or speech how sleep, food, and movement affect how I feel.
criteria.criterion_label: Health impact explanation in writing or speech

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band B.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I mention sleep, food, or movement but do not explain how each affects how I feel, or I use a generic statement ('they make me feel good').
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I explain how one of sleep, food, or movement affects how I feel but only partly cover the others, or my explanations for different factors all sound the same.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can explain in writing or speech how sleep, food, and movement affect how I feel.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I name a time when two of these factors interacted — for example, bad sleep made food choices harder — and explain the effect.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Two distinct cognitive operations: explaining causal relationships versus goal-setting behavior require separate assessment.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can set one specific healthy-habit goal using a template.
criteria.criterion_label: Specific habit goal with template

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band B.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I fill in the template but my habit goal is generic ('be healthier') or it names a wish rather than a specific action I would do.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: My goal is specific but a part of the template (trigger, duration, or review point) is not filled in or does not match the rest of the goal.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can set one specific healthy-habit goal using a template.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: My goal includes a specific condition under which I would know it is working, not only that I did it.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Two distinct cognitive operations: explaining causal relationships versus goal-setting behavior require separate assessment.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can track one habit for a week using a paper log or Strive.
criteria.criterion_label: Week-long habit tracking

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band C.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I track the habit on the first day or two but the log has gaps after that, or my tracking records intention rather than what actually happened.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I track the habit across most of the week but miss a day without noting why, or my log shows the action without showing the context.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can track one habit for a week using a paper log or Strive.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: My tracking notes one factor beyond the habit itself (time of day, mood, what happened just before) so the log shows a pattern, not just a count.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Tracking represents a distinct data collection skill that can be assessed independently through record-keeping evidence.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can write a short analysis identifying one specific barrier to my habit consistency.
criteria.criterion_label: Written barrier identification

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band C.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: My analysis says the habit was hard but does not name what specifically got in the way, or names a barrier that is actually a general excuse.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I name a barrier but describe it at a general level ('I was busy') rather than pinpointing the specific situation that caused the break.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can write a short analysis identifying one specific barrier to my habit consistency.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I name a barrier that I did not see at the start of the week and that showed up only because I was tracking.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Barrier identification requires analytical thinking that is separate from tracking or explanation skills.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can explain why I want to keep or change the habit I have been tracking.
criteria.criterion_label: Habit continuation reasoning

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band C.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I say whether I want to keep or change the habit but do not give a reason, or my reason is 'because I should.'
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I give a reason but do not connect it to what my tracking actually showed this week, or my reason would have been the same without the tracking.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can explain why I want to keep or change the habit I have been tracking.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I distinguish between what the habit did for me and what I hoped it would do, and explain my decision based on that difference.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Explanation requires reflective reasoning skills distinct from data collection and barrier analysis, representing a separate cognitive operation.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can write a comparative analysis of two health information sources.
criteria.criterion_label: Written health source comparison

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band D.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: My analysis summarises each health source separately but does not compare them on shared criteria.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I compare the sources on one criterion (e.g. who wrote it) but do not apply the comparison to other criteria like evidence cited or funding.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can write a comparative analysis of two health information sources.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I compare the sources on a dimension they do not openly advertise — for example, what each source does not discuss.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Comparing sources requires distinct evaluation skills separate from planning and adjusting habits.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can create a balanced weekly habits plan that accounts for known obstacles I am likely to encounter.
criteria.criterion_label: Obstacle-aware weekly habits plan

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band D.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: My plan lists habits but does not name any obstacles, or it names obstacles but does not build them into the plan.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I name obstacles and build around one of them but not the others, or my plan addresses obstacles I have not actually encountered.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can create a balanced weekly habits plan that accounts for known obstacles I am likely to encounter.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: My plan names an obstacle I cannot fully remove and specifies what I will do differently on days when it shows up.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Creating balanced plans requires synthesis skills distinct from information evaluation and plan adjustment.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can document adjustments I have made to my habits plan based on logged results.
criteria.criterion_label: Results-based plan adjustment documentation

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band D.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I document that I changed the plan but not which log entries drove the change, or my adjustments do not match the pattern in the log.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I document an adjustment based on results but only one, or the adjustment responds to a single bad day rather than a pattern.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can document adjustments I have made to my habits plan based on logged results.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I document an adjustment that changes the plan's structure (when I do the habit) rather than only its content (what the habit is).

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Plan adjustment requires data analysis and reflection skills that are cognitively distinct from creating initial plans.

criteria.criterion_statement: The student names a specific trusted adult they could go to for help and carries out a simple self-care routine with supportive reminders, across most occasions when it is needed.
criteria.criterion_label: [Band A] Self-Care & Resilience (dispositional observation)
criteria.decomposition_rationale: This is a single dispositional criterion combining help-seeking and self-care behaviours, as both reflect the same underlying wellbeing orientation observable at this developmental level.
observation_indicators: The student names a specific trusted adult (parent, class teacher, school counsellor) they could go to for help, unprompted.
The student carries out a simple self-care routine (handwashing, toothbrushing, water, rest) with supportive reminders, on most occasions it is needed.
The student asks for help from a trusted adult in small everyday matters, not only in big or visible distress moments.
confusable_behaviours: Help-seeking only at crisis (waiting for visible distress before asking) mistaken for healthy help-seeking. Distinction: the disposition is help-seeking as an ordinary skill, not an emergency reach.
Tattling (seeking adult intervention against a peer) mistaken for trusted-adult relationship. Distinction: help-seeking here is for the student's own wellbeing, not adjudication of peer disputes.
absence_indicators: The student waits for visible physical distress (fever, tears, injury) before asking for help, indicating help-seeking is not yet a normal skill.
The student does not have a consistent specific trusted adult named outside their immediate family, over weeks of classroom life.
conversation_prompts: Who is a grown-up at school you can ask for help when you need it? Tell me about a time you did.
Tell me about what happens when you feel not-quite-right — what do you do?
Tell me about a time you asked for help and it made something better.

criteria.criterion_statement: The student names a bodily early-warning sign in themselves and selects a practised self-care response before activation escalates, across multiple occasions.
criteria.criterion_label: [Band B] Self-Care & Resilience (dispositional observation)
criteria.decomposition_rationale: This is a single dispositional behaviour combining body awareness with self-care response selection, observable as one integrated capability.
observation_indicators: The student names a bodily early-warning sign in themselves (tiredness, irritability, headache, tight chest) in their own words.
The student selects a practised self-care response (water, short break, movement, feeling-naming, calm corner) before activation fully escalates, across multiple occasions.
The student signals to an adult when they have noticed an early sign they do not yet have a response for, rather than waiting for the sign to become unmanageable.
confusable_behaviours: After-the-fact narration ("I was tired so I got grumpy") mistaken for early self-care. Distinction: the disposition is pre-escalation noticing and response, not retrospective explanation.

Generic tiredness reporting (saying "I'm tired" as a regular refrain without changed behaviour) mistaken for signal-responsive care. Distinction: the noticing is followed by an action calibrated to what the student noticed.
absence_indicators: Escalation episodes (tears, snapping at a peer, withdrawal) recur weekly with no observable pre-escalation pause or response.
The student can name signals in abstract (lessons on the body) but does not apply the vocabulary to their own in-the-moment state.
conversation_prompts: What are the first signs in your body that tell you something's building up?
Tell me about a time you caught a feeling early this month — what did you do?
When do you notice signs early, and when do they sneak up on you?

criteria.criterion_statement: The student articulates a self-care plan with context-specific strategies for home and school, names multiple trusted people or services for support, and acts on the plan across real situations.
criteria.criterion_label: [Band C] Self-Care & Resilience (dispositional observation)
criteria.decomposition_rationale: This is a single dispositional criterion as it represents one integrated behaviour of planning and help-seeking that demonstrates the learner's developing self-care capacity across contexts.
observation_indicators: The student articulates — spontaneously or on brief prompt — a self-care plan with context-specific strategies for both home and school.
The student names two or more trusted people or services (counsellor, teacher, parent, nurse, external line) they could turn to for support, with specificity about which for which kind of concern.
The student's plan acknowledges that different contexts (home, school, online) need different strategies, rather than applying one strategy everywhere.
The student follows through on their plan in real situations, not only in reflective conversation.
confusable_behaviours: Plan fluency (being able to describe the plan on request) mistaken for enacted self-care. Distinction: observation in real stressful moments should match the plan's claims.

Listing adults from a poster (reciting the help-seeking chain) mistaken for having a genuine trusted adult. Distinction: the disposition shows in specificity — which adult for which concern, not just "a teacher".
absence_indicators: The student can name strategies and people but the real-situation response shows no deployment of either, over multiple observed occasions.
The student's plan flattens home and school into the same thing, indicating they have not yet grasped that contexts differ.
conversation_prompts: Where do you find self-care easiest — at home or at school — and why?
When do you find it easiest to ask someone for help, and when does it feel hardest?
Tell me about a plan you have for when a hard day is starting — does it work?

criteria.criterion_statement: The student sustains key self-care routines through genuinely stressful periods without adult scaffolding, and offers non-directive support to a peer at least occasionally.
criteria.criterion_label: [Band D] Self-Care & Resilience (dispositional observation)
criteria.decomposition_rationale: This is a single dispositional criterion as it represents one integrated behaviour pattern of resilient self-care that includes both personal maintenance and peer support elements observable together.
observation_indicators: The student sustains key self-care routines (sleep protection, movement, exit from pressurising group chat, calm corner, food and water) through a genuinely stressful period — assessment window, social friction, demanding project — without adult scaffolding.
The student's self-care pattern under pressure holds consistently with their self-care pattern in calm periods, rather than collapsing when it is most needed.
The student offers a peer a specific non-directive form of support (sitting alongside, asking what they need, gentle checking in) on at least one observed occasion.
The student seeks help when they recognise their own strategies are insufficient, rather than persisting alone past the point they can manage.
confusable_behaviours: Performed resilience ("I'm fine") under visible strain mistaken for sustained self-care. Distinction: the disposition is observable practice continuing — not the absence of complaint.
Directive peer help ("you should try X") mistaken for non-directive support. Distinction: non-directive support asks what the peer needs, does not prescribe.
absence_indicators: Self-care routines collapse first in pressure weeks (sleep drops, exercise stops, food becomes irregular) even though the student has the capability in calm weeks.
Under pressure, the student takes on peer support in a way that overextends them (the "helper" role as a way to avoid their own self-care).
conversation_prompts: Which parts of your self-care hold up under pressure, and which fall off first?
Tell me about a time this term you helped a friend who was struggling — how did you decide what to do?
What does 'looking after yourself' actually mean for you right now?

criteria.criterion_statement: I can name private body parts using correct terminology in a short scenario-response task.
criteria.criterion_label: Body part terminology in scenario task

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band A.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I name some body parts but use inaccurate words for private parts, or I avoid naming private body parts altogether in the scenario response.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I use correct terminology in most parts of the task but revert to an informal or inaccurate word for at least one private body part.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can name private body parts using correct terminology in a short scenario-response task.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I use the correct terminology in the scenario and also recognise when an adult in the scenario uses an incorrect term.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Two distinct criteria are needed as naming body parts requires factual knowledge while identifying trusted adults involves relationship awareness and safety understanding.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can complete a written scenario analysis identifying whether consent was freely given, unclear, or overridden by pressure.
criteria.criterion_label: Consent scenario analysis

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band B.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: My analysis of the scenario says whether the person said yes or no but does not judge whether the yes was freely given, unclear, or pressured.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I identify whether consent was freely given or overridden but confuse 'unclear' and 'overridden,' or my reasoning does not point to the specific detail in the scenario that made the difference.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can complete a written scenario analysis identifying whether consent was freely given, unclear, or overridden by pressure.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I identify a detail in the scenario (a smaller cue, a silence, a change of tone) that tipped the situation from freely given into unclear.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Setting and respecting boundaries involves distinct but related skills that can be assessed together as they represent the same cognitive operation applied to boundary management.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can describe what a clear request and a clear answer sound like in a consent situation.
criteria.criterion_label: Clear consent communication description

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band B.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I describe something someone said in a consent situation but my description does not distinguish a clear request from a vague or pressured one.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I describe either a clear request or a clear answer but not both, or my description leaves out the specific language that makes the request or answer clear.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can describe what a clear request and a clear answer sound like in a consent situation.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I describe a request-and-answer pair and note what would change if either side became less clear.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Asking for consent represents a distinct cognitive operation from boundary management, requiring different communication and decision-making skills.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can describe what a help-seeking response sounds like when consent has been overridden or is unclear.
criteria.criterion_label: Help-seeking response description

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band B.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I say someone should get help when consent has been overridden but do not describe what the help-seeking response sounds like in words.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I describe what a help-seeking response sounds like, but it is the same in all cases rather than adjusted to whether consent was unclear or overridden.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can describe what a help-seeking response sounds like when consent has been overridden or is unclear.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I distinguish a help-seeking response for 'unclear' from one for 'overridden' and can say why they are different.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Help-seeking when pressured involves different skills than boundary-setting or consent-asking, including recognising pressure situations and knowing how to access support.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can complete a scenario-response task that applies consent reasoning to non-sexual, physical, and online scenarios.
criteria.criterion_label: Consent reasoning across contexts

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band C.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I apply consent reasoning to one of the three scenario types (non-sexual, physical, or online) but not to the others, or I use very different reasoning for each.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I apply consent reasoning to all three scenario types but the reasoning for one of them (usually online) is weaker or less specific than for the others.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can complete a scenario-response task that applies consent reasoning to non-sexual, physical, and online scenarios.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I apply consent reasoning across the three scenarios and note which scenario type makes consent hardest to read, and why.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: This represents a distinct cognitive operation of applying procedural knowledge in specific contexts.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can state a specific clear boundary to a peer in a structured roleplay.
criteria.criterion_label: Specific boundary statement in roleplay

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band C.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I state a boundary in the roleplay but the statement is vague ('that's not OK'), or I address the person's behaviour rather than naming the specific limit.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: My boundary statement is specific but delivered as a complaint or explanation rather than as a clear limit, or I soften it with 'maybe' or 'sorry.'
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can state a specific clear boundary to a peer in a structured roleplay.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: My boundary statement is specific and names what I will do if the peer continues, not only what I am asking them to stop.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Boundary communication is a separate cognitive operation from applying consent skills, requiring different communication competencies.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can complete a written analysis of a pressure scenario that names the specific power and peer-pressure dynamics at play.
criteria.criterion_label: Power dynamics written analysis

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band D.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: My analysis of the pressure scenario describes what happened but does not name any power or peer-pressure dynamic.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I name a power or peer-pressure dynamic but do not connect it to specific features of the scenario (who had authority, who had an audience, who had information).
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can complete a written analysis of a pressure scenario that names the specific power and peer-pressure dynamics at play.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I identify a power dynamic that is not obvious on first read — for example, whose silence is giving the pressurer cover.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Recognition of risks is a distinct cognitive skill from planning responses or supporting others.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can describe how I would support a peer to access confidential help when they face pressure or safety concerns.
criteria.criterion_label: Peer confidential help-seeking support

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band D.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I describe helping the peer but do not describe how I would support them to access confidential help specifically, or I describe telling an adult on their behalf.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I describe supporting the peer to access help but miss the confidentiality dimension — I name who they should tell but not how to protect their privacy.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can describe how I would support a peer to access confidential help when they face pressure or safety concerns.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I distinguish between the support the peer needs right now and the confidentiality decisions that are theirs to make, not mine.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Supporting others requires distinct interpersonal and help-seeking knowledge beyond personal safety skills.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can complete a written or oral explanation identifying at least five physical and two emotional changes of puberty.
criteria.criterion_label: Puberty changes identification

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band B.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I name some physical changes of puberty but fewer than five, or I name changes without distinguishing physical from emotional.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I name five physical changes and one or two emotional ones, but the emotional changes are generic ('moody') rather than specific.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can complete a written or oral explanation identifying at least five physical and two emotional changes of puberty.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I describe a puberty change that is less commonly discussed (e.g. changing sleep needs, body image, energy patterns) in addition to the standard list.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: This targets the specific cognitive act of explaining puberty changes, which is distinct from the procedural skills that follow.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can describe an appropriate personal hygiene routine in writing or speech.
criteria.criterion_label: Puberty hygiene routine description

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band B.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I describe a hygiene routine but it is a pre-puberty routine, or my description skips the changes puberty brings that require new steps.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: My routine includes puberty-specific steps but misses one (e.g. covers body but not hair or teeth changes), or the steps are listed without any reasoning.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can describe an appropriate personal hygiene routine in writing or speech.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: My description notes how the routine has to adapt when a puberty change (sweat, acne, menstruation) actually starts, not only in anticipation.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: This represents a procedural skill that is cognitively distinct from explaining concepts or finding information.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can name two reliable health information sources for puberty questions.
criteria.criterion_label: Reliable puberty information sources

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band B.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I name one source for puberty questions but not a second, or I name two but one is not reliable (a friend, a random site).
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I name two reliable sources but they are the same type (both websites, or both adults), or I cannot say why I count them as reliable.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can name two reliable health information sources for puberty questions.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I can say what kind of puberty question each source is best suited to, and why I would not use the same source for everything.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Information literacy represents a distinct cognitive operation from explaining concepts or applying procedures, requiring evaluation and selection skills.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can describe the hygiene routines relevant to my own pubertal development in writing or speech.
criteria.criterion_label: Pubertal hygiene routine description

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band C.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: My description is about puberty hygiene in general rather than relevant to my own development, or I describe steps I do not currently need.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I describe my relevant hygiene routine but miss a step that applies to my current stage, or my reasoning for each step is generic rather than personal.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can describe the hygiene routines relevant to my own pubertal development in writing or speech.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: My description notes which part of my routine I expect to change next as my development continues.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: This criterion focuses on the practical application and management of hygiene routines, which is distinct from the cognitive tasks of identifying sources and explaining reproduction.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can apply stated criteria to distinguish a trusted health source from an unreliable one in a short source-comparison task.
criteria.criterion_label: Criteria-based source distinction

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band C.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I say which source I trust more but do not apply the stated criteria, or I apply them but only to one of the two sources.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I apply the stated criteria to both sources but the reasoning is thin on one criterion — for example, I check who wrote it but not what evidence it cites.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can apply stated criteria to distinguish a trusted health source from an unreliable one in a short source-comparison task.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I apply the criteria and identify a case where two criteria disagree (a well-credentialed author on an unreliable platform) and handle the conflict.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Source identification requires distinct evaluative skills separate from hygiene management and reproductive knowledge.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can complete a written explanation of human reproduction at an age-appropriate level.
criteria.criterion_label: Written reproduction explanation

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band C.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: My explanation names part of reproduction (one system, or the act only) without covering how the process works end to end at my level.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I explain reproduction at an age-appropriate level but one step is missing or oversimplified in a way that could mislead, or the language slips between technical and vague.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can complete a written explanation of human reproduction at an age-appropriate level.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: My explanation covers the process and also notes something it does not explain on its own — for example, what decides when reproduction is biologically possible.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Explaining reproduction involves different cognitive demands (factual recall and conceptual understanding) than the practical and evaluative skills in the other criteria.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can complete a written scenario-based risk-assessment task covering STI, pregnancy, and substance-use scenarios.
criteria.criterion_label: Written health risk scenario assessment

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band D.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: My response names a risk but does not assess it, or covers only one of the three scenario types (STI, pregnancy, substance-use).
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I assess risk across the three scenarios but my reasoning applies the same logic everywhere, rather than what is specific to each kind of risk.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can complete a written scenario-based risk-assessment task covering STI, pregnancy, and substance-use scenarios.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I identify how risks across the three types can compound (e.g. how substance use changes the likelihood of unprotected sex) and factor that into my assessment.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Risk assessment is a distinct cognitive operation requiring evaluation and analysis skills separate from explaining protection methods.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can identify the relevant protection methods for each of the risk scenarios.
criteria.criterion_label: Scenario-matched protection methods

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band D.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I list protection methods but do not match them to the scenarios, or I match only one scenario to a method.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I match protection methods to each risk scenario but do not distinguish methods that reduce risk from methods that prevent it, or I over-attribute what a single method can do.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can identify the relevant protection methods for each of the risk scenarios.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I identify a scenario where a single method is not enough and more than one protection is needed, and say why.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Explaining protection methods requires different cognitive skills from risk assessment and involves declarative knowledge application.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can describe specific steps for accessing confidential health advice in this jurisdiction.
criteria.criterion_label: Jurisdictional confidential health access steps

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band D.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: My steps are generic ('go to a doctor') rather than specific to this jurisdiction, or I describe access without noting the confidentiality conditions.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I describe the access steps for this jurisdiction but confuse the confidentiality conditions (e.g. what can be shared at what age), or the steps miss a gatekeeper a young person would actually meet.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can describe specific steps for accessing confidential health advice in this jurisdiction.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I name a condition under which the default confidentiality in this jurisdiction would not apply, and what a young person could do then.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Identifying access steps involves procedural knowledge distinct from risk assessment and protection explanation, requiring knowledge of health systems and services.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can take turns in conversation in a short observed roleplay, waiting for others to finish speaking before I respond.
criteria.criterion_label: Turn-taking in observed roleplay

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band A.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I begin to speak while the other person is still speaking, or I wait but drift off-topic and do not pick up the exchange.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I wait for others to finish most of the time but interrupt at predictable points — for instance, when I am excited or when I think I know the answer.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can take turns in conversation in a short observed roleplay, waiting for others to finish speaking before I respond.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I wait, and when there is a gap, notice whether the other person is actually finished or just pausing before I step in.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Taking turns, using kind words, and making I-statements are three distinct interpersonal communication skills that can be independently assessed and developed.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can use kind language in my communication with others.
criteria.criterion_label: Kind language in communication

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band A.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I use kind language with some peers or on some occasions but slip into unkind language when I am frustrated or impatient.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: My language is kind across most of the exchange, but tone or volume contradicts the words, or I am kind to one person and less kind to another in the same activity.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can use kind language in my communication with others.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I notice when unkind language would be faster or funnier and choose kind language anyway, and can say why.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Taking turns, using kind words, and making I-statements are three distinct interpersonal communication skills that can be independently assessed and developed.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can use an I-statement to state a specific need in a structured roleplay or scenario-response.
criteria.criterion_label: I-statement for specific need expression

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band B.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: My I-statement names a feeling but does not name a specific need, or names a need so general it could apply to any scenario.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: My I-statement names a need specific to the scenario, but it is stated as a complaint or a wish rather than as a request the other person could act on.
